issues.description: The document contains two separate introduction sections. Section sec_28be1dbc4119 contains an Abstract (sec_7cfab129da38) that provides comprehensive framing of the research. Section sec_86962f8f508d then provides another full Introduction with five paragraphs that largely restate the same framing information: significance of PETM, research questions, scope, and thematic organization. Both sections establish context, research questions, and thematic structure in overlapping ways.
issues.action_details: Merge the two introductory sections. The Abstract should remain as a standalone summary. The Introduction section (sec_86962f8f508d) should be consolidated to eliminate redundancy with the Abstract. Consider removing or significantly condensing blk_c3fe018bbc36 and blk_87697f760207 as they duplicate information already in the Abstract about PETM significance and scientific value.

issues.description: The Conclusions section (sec_e3d2388df512) lacks substantive synthesis of the six research themes analyzed throughout the document. Instead of synthesizing findings about volcanic sources, methane hydrates, chronology, modeling, ocean responses, and weathering feedbacks, the conclusion provides only generic statements about research themes, methodological diversity, and ongoing debates. It does not answer the three research questions posed in the Introduction or provide integrated insights across the thematic areas.
issues.action_details: Expand the Conclusions section to synthesize specific findings from each of the six thematic sections. Answer the three research questions explicitly: (1) what are the main research themes (with specific findings from each), (2) what methodological approaches were employed (with evaluation of their strengths and limitations), and (3) what are key findings and areas of disagreement (with specific evidence from the reviewed literature). Connect findings across themes to show how they interrelate.

issues.description: Section sec_28be1dbc4119 has a heading 'Literature Review: the causes of the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM), sometimes also spelled Palaeocene' but contains only an Abstract subsection and an empty '1. Introduction' subsection heading (sec_ad4841dbf6b2). This creates structural confusion as the section promises content it doesn't deliver. The actual introduction content appears in sec_86962f8f508d.
issues.action_details: Remove the outer wrapper section sec_28be1dbc4119 or restructure to eliminate the empty subsection sec_ad4841dbf6b2. The Abstract should either be a standalone section at the document level or the first subsection of a properly organized introductory section.

issues.description: Discussion of the relationship between volcanic and methane hydrate sources is scattered across three non-adjacent sections. Section sec_fea38630b257 (Volcanic and Magmatic Carbon Sources) discusses this in blk_c31aa593a5b9, section sec_94cee2542b14 (Methane Hydrate Dissociation) addresses it in blk_41149f2e43d9, and section sec_cbafd124c457 (Discussion) revisits it extensively in blk_1166149f6b30 and blk_a0ffb2112ba8. This fragmentation makes it difficult to understand the integrated view of how these sources interact.
issues.action_details: Consolidate discussion of volcanic-methane interactions primarily in the Discussion section where it is most appropriately synthesized. Remove or significantly reduce the forward-looking speculation about the relationship from the individual thematic sections (blk_c31aa593a5b9 and blk_41149f2e43d9), allowing those sections to focus on their specific domains. The Discussion section should be the primary location for synthesis of competing and complementary hypotheses.

issues.description: Block blk_d0c898e89d35 in the Introduction describes the scope of the review (focusing on causes rather than consequences, global geographic scope, timeframe). Block blk_cdddb487a6bd in the Methodology section repeats much of this same scope information, stating that it examines causes of PETM and encompasses 1995-2025 literature. This is redundant framing that appears in close succession.
issues.action_details: Remove the scope description from the Methodology section (blk_cdddb487a6bd) since it is already established in the Introduction. The Methodology section should begin directly with the search strategy details without restating what was already covered.

issues.description: The document ends abruptly with a generic statement about urgency for informing climate projections in blk_698395da9e5d, followed immediately by an extensive References section. There is no synthesizing conclusion that brings together the findings from the six research themes, no clear statement about what has been learned from reviewing 51 papers, and no forward-looking perspective beyond the generic statement. The document lacks closure.
issues.action_details: Add substantive concluding paragraphs after blk_698395da9e5d that: (1) synthesize the key findings across the six themes, (2) clearly state what the body of literature reveals about PETM causation, (3) identify the most significant remaining uncertainties, and (4) provide specific recommendations for future research directions. This should answer the research questions posed at the beginning and provide intellectual closure.

issues.description: The transition from the Introduction section (sec_86962f8f508d) to the Methodology section (sec_5751f6c42437) is abrupt. Block blk_ad639fb4d836 ends with discussion of thematic organization and research development, but there is no transitional statement preparing the reader for the methodological discussion that follows. The presence of an empty heading 'sec_5721579cefba' (2. Methodology) at the end of the Introduction section further indicates structural confusion.
issues.action_details: Add a brief transitional sentence at the end of blk_ad639fb4d836 that signals the shift from substantive framing to methodological description. Something like: 'The following section details the systematic methodology employed to identify, select, and synthesize this body of literature.' Also remove the orphaned heading sec_5721579cefba.

issues.description: Each major thematic section (sections 3-8) has an empty subsection heading at the end pointing to the next section. For example, sec_fea38630b257 ends with empty heading 'sec_306a60774212' pointing to section 4. These empty headings serve no purpose and create orphaned structural elements that clutter the document organization.
issues.action_details: Remove all empty subsection headings that appear at the end of major sections: sec_2bb9cc0fca46, sec_1ea6f88d6854, sec_3f9d25d0c161, sec_a0c77f5f4dd8, sec_685f9b2edd75, sec_ae907dbda921, sec_2ef65863be42, sec_9b7059fb5ecd. These serve no navigational purpose and interrupt the document flow.

issues.description: The Discussion section (sec_cbafd124c457) and Conclusions section (sec_e3d2388df512) have overlapping content and unclear functional distinction. The Discussion provides synthesis about trigger mechanisms versus system dynamics, methodological implications, and theoretical advances. The Conclusions then attempts to cover similar ground with research themes, methodological landscape, and key findings. Both sections touch on debates, consensus, and implications but without clear differentiation of purpose.
issues.action_details: Consider consolidating Discussion and Conclusions into a single 'Discussion and Conclusions' section, or clearly differentiate their roles. The Discussion should synthesize findings and address debates (which it does well). The Conclusions should provide crisp answers to the research questions, state what the literature review accomplished, and offer concrete future directions. Currently, there is substantial functional overlap that dilutes both sections.

issues.description: The Discussion section begins directly with substantive synthesis (blk_a0112bc179b0) without any framing statement about what the Discussion section will accomplish or how it relates to the preceding thematic sections. The reader transitions abruptly from detailed weathering feedback content to high-level synthesis without preparation.
issues.action_details: Add a brief introductory paragraph before blk_a0112bc179b0 that frames the Discussion section's purpose: to synthesize findings across the six thematic areas, evaluate the state of knowledge about PETM causation, and identify key theoretical and methodological implications. This would help readers understand the shift from descriptive review to integrative analysis.

edits.introduction_requirements: Add a brief introductory paragraph before blk_a0112bc179b0 that frames the Discussion section's purpose: to synthesize findings across the six thematic areas, evaluate the state of knowledge about PETM causation, and identify key theoretical and methodological implications. This would help readers understand the shift from descriptive review to integrative analysis.

edits.conclusion_requirements: Add substantive concluding paragraphs after blk_698395da9e5d that: (1) synthesize the key findings across the six themes, (2) clearly state what the body of literature reveals about PETM causation, (3) identify the most significant remaining uncertainties, and (4) provide specific recommendations for future research directions. This should answer the research questions posed at the beginning and provide intellectual closure.

completed_edits.generated_content:
Understanding the causes of the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum requires synthesizing evidence across multiple disciplines and methodological frameworks. The 56-million-year-old event left an unmistakable geochemical fingerprint in sedimentary records worldwide—a dramatic negative carbon isotope excursion accompanied by sustained global warming lasting approximately 170,000 years. Yet the precise mechanisms triggering this hyperthermal episode, and the relative contributions of potential carbon sources, remain subjects of active scientific debate. The six thematic domains identified in this review—volcanic and magmatic activity, methane hydrate dissociation, carbon isotope stratigraphy, carbon cycle modeling, ocean biogeochemistry, and silicate weathering feedbacks—represent interconnected approaches to resolving this paleoclimatic puzzle.

What makes the PETM causation question particularly consequential is its relevance to contemporary climate concerns. The event provides a natural experiment in how Earth systems respond to rapid carbon injection under warm baseline conditions, offering critical insights into feedback mechanisms, climate sensitivity, and ecosystem resilience. However, extracting these lessons requires moving beyond isolated disciplinary findings toward an integrated understanding of how different causal mechanisms may have operated simultaneously or sequentially. Some researchers propose methane release as the primary trigger, while others emphasize large-scale volcanism associated with the North Atlantic Igneous Province. Still others suggest orbital forcing, organic matter oxidation, or complex interactions among these processes. The following sections evaluate evidence supporting each hypothesis, examine methodological strengths and limitations across research domains, and explore how integrating these perspectives advances our understanding of both ancient climate dynamics and the Earth system's response to contemporary carbon perturbations.

The Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum represents one of paleoclimate science's most intensively studied rapid warming events precisely because its causes remain incompletely resolved despite five decades of research. This systematic review of 51 papers demonstrates that the scientific community has converged on certain fundamental truths while simultaneously revealing the limits of current evidence to definitively answer the central question: what triggered this hyperthermal event 56 million years ago?

The synthesis of six major research themes establishes that PETM causation was almost certainly multifactorial, involving some combination of carbon release—whether from methane hydrates, organic matter oxidation, or volcanic outgassing—coupled with positive feedback mechanisms that amplified initial warming through reduced weathering rates, ocean stratification, and diminished carbon sequestration. The methodological pluralism evident across these studies strengthens confidence in this broad framework, as independent geochemical, stratigraphic, modeling, and paleontological approaches converge on similar conclusions about event magnitude and global extent.

However, equifinality emerges as the field's most fundamental challenge. Multiple plausible combinations of trigger mechanisms and feedback strengths can produce observationally indistinguishable outcomes within current proxy resolution and chronological uncertainties. Rather than viewing this as a deficit, the field should recognize that quantifying irreducible uncertainties is itself a significant scientific achievement that calibrates realistic expectations for paleoclimate reconstruction.

Future progress requires three integrated approaches. First, expanding high-resolution terrestrial archives with improved chronological control will reduce bias toward marine carbonate records. Second, coupled Earth system models must simultaneously constrain carbon cycle dynamics, climate physics, ocean biogeochemistry, and diagenetic processes rather than treating them independently. Third, paleocene boundary condition quantification—particularly methane hydrate distributions and baseline ocean chemistry—must narrow the parameter space within which models operate.

Ultimately, PETM research exemplifies how ancient climate events illuminate both the complexity of Earth system responses to rapid carbon perturbations and the necessity of embracing scientific uncertainty. As contemporary emissions accelerate, this hard-won understanding of past climate dynamics becomes increasingly critical for informing policy decisions and climate projections.

This systematic review reveals that PETM causation research has crystallized around six interconnected research themes that collectively construct a multidimensional understanding of this critical paleoclimate event. The dominant theme addressing carbon source identification demonstrates that while massive carbon release is universally accepted as the primary forcing mechanism, considerable debate persists regarding the specific source(s). Research spanning methane hydrate dissociation, organic matter combustion, and volcanic outgassing—exemplified by Liu et al.'s assessment of comet and volcanic contributions and Thomas et al.'s thermal dissociation evidence—reveals that single-source hypotheses increasingly give way to polycausal models. This thematic convergence suggests that the PETM likely resulted from multiple carbon reservoirs being mobilized through complex feedback interactions, a conclusion that gains support when integrated with themes addressing trigger mechanisms and feedback processes.

The methodological pluralism evident across these 51 papers represents a considerable strength, enabling robust cross-validation through independent analytical pathways. Geochemical proxy systems, particularly stable isotope analysis documented in studies by Sluijs and Dickels and Handley et al., provide direct evidence of carbon cycle perturbations, while paleontological approaches offer independent constraints on environmental changes. Climate modeling contributions from Panchuk et al. and Carozza et al. translate proxy evidence into mechanistic understanding, demonstrating how initial perturbations propagated through Earth system feedbacks. However, this methodological diversity also reveals critical limitations: temporal resolution remains a persistent constraint, with disagreements over PETM duration between high-resolution records and model simulations creating uncertainty in trigger identification. Furthermore, the spatial heterogeneity of records—concentrated in North Atlantic and Pacific archives—introduces potential regional bias into supposedly global interpretations.

The integration of findings across research themes illuminates previously opaque connections. Volcanic activity identified through lithium isotope analysis and North Atlantic seaway connectivity, documented by Jones et al., operates not merely as a direct carbon source but potentially as a trigger mechanism that destabilized methane hydrate reservoirs—a mechanism that cannot be understood from single-source perspectives alone. Similarly, enhanced weathering responses identified by Ramos et al. and Pogge von Strandmann et al. represent feedback mechanisms that necessarily followed initial carbon release, yet these feedbacks fundamentally shaped the duration and magnitude of temperature anomalies. Zeebe et al.'s demonstration that carbon dioxide forcing alone cannot explain observed warming underscores how individual research themes, when isolated, remain incomplete; only through synthesis do trigger mechanisms, carbon sources, and feedback processes reveal their mutual dependencies.

These interconnections highlight why resolving PETM causation requires not merely accumulating evidence within existing themes but fundamentally reconceptualizing how these dimensions interact across timescales ranging from rapid initial perturbations to protracted recovery phases. This synthetic perspective positions contemporary climate research to anticipate similarly complex responses to ongoing carbon perturbations.

Having established the thematic framework that has structured decades of PETM research, understanding how these six interconnected areas were synthesized requires explicit examination of the methodological approach employed in this review. Rather than treating volcanic inputs, methane dynamics, isotopic constraints, modeling efforts, ocean responses, and weathering feedbacks as isolated domains, this analysis adopts an integrated perspective that traces how evidence across disciplines converges on—or diverges from—competing hypotheses about event causation. The following section details the systematic procedures used to identify, evaluate, and synthesize the peer-reviewed literature underlying this interdisciplinary synthesis, thereby establishing the evidentiary foundation upon which subsequent thematic discussions rest.

completed_edits.consolidated_content:
The Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum (PETM) represents one of the most dramatic and rapid episodes of global warming in Earth's geological history. Occurring approximately 56 million years ago, this event witnessed a global temperature increase of 5-8°C within several thousand years, accompanied by massive carbon release into the ocean-atmosphere system, widespread ocean acidification, and profound ecological reorganization across both terrestrial and marine environments. The PETM stands as a natural experiment of exceptional scientific value, offering crucial insights into how the Earth system responds to rapid carbon cycle perturbations under greenhouse conditions. As contemporary society grapples with anthropogenic climate change, understanding the mechanisms, feedbacks, and consequences of this ancient hyperthermal event has become increasingly relevant to projecting future climate trajectories and tipping points.

The scientific significance of the PETM extends beyond its role as a paleoclimate analog for modern warming. This event presents a unique opportunity to investigate fundamental questions about carbon cycle dynamics, climate sensitivity, biogeochemical feedbacks, and ecosystem resilience under extreme environmental stress. The carbon isotope excursion associated with the PETM—a distinctive negative shift in δ¹³C values preserved in marine and terrestrial sediments worldwide—provides an unambiguous stratigraphic marker and quantitative constraint on carbon sources and fluxes. However, despite three decades of intensive research since the event's initial characterization in the early 1990s, fundamental questions regarding the triggering mechanisms, carbon sources, and cascade of feedbacks remain subjects of active scientific debate.

This systematic literature review synthesizes 51 peer-reviewed publications from 1995-2025 to examine the causes and mechanisms of this hyperthermal event and addresses three primary research questions. First, what are the main research themes that have emerged in investigations of PETM causation? Second, what methodological approaches have researchers employed to reconstruct and understand this ancient warming event? Third, what are the key findings and persistent areas of scientific disagreement regarding the mechanisms responsible for initiating and sustaining the PETM? Through thematic analysis, six major research domains were identified: volcanic and magmatic carbon sources, methane hydrate dissociation and feedback mechanisms, carbon isotope stratigraphy and chronological constraints, carbon cycle modeling, ocean biogeochemical responses, and silicate weathering feedbacks. The review reveals that researchers employ diverse methodological approaches including geochemical proxy analyses, stratigraphic techniques, climate modeling, and paleontological methods. While broad consensus exists regarding massive carbon release as the primary driver, the specific carbon source—whether methane hydrates, volcanic emissions, organic matter combustion, or combined mechanisms—remains debated. Resolving these uncertainties has significant implications for understanding Earth system dynamics and projecting responses to contemporary anthropogenic carbon emissions, particularly regarding feedback mechanisms and climate sensitivity under rapid warming scenarios.

Despite substantial progress in characterizing both volcanic and methane hydrate carbon sources, fundamental uncertainties persist that complicate our understanding of their relative contributions and interactions during the PETM. The precise quantification of carbon released through different volcanic mechanisms—direct magmatic degassing, thermogenic methane from contact metamorphism, hydrothermal venting, and subcrustal mobilization—remains elusive due to challenges in constraining the spatial extent of intrusions, the organic carbon content of affected sediments, and the efficiency of carbon transfer from sediments to atmosphere. Similarly, quantification of Paleocene methane hydrate reservoir sizes and locations remains poorly constrained, making it difficult to assess whether available reservoirs were sufficient to explain the full magnitude of the CIE. The heterogeneity in magma chemistry within individual LIPs further complicates efforts to extrapolate from limited geochemical measurements to total carbon budgets, highlighting the need for extensive sampling across the full temporal and spatial extent of NAIP magmatism.

Better temporal constraints on individual volcanic phases relative to the detailed structure of the CIE onset would clarify whether carbon release occurred as a single massive pulse or through multiple episodes, while improved constraints on the isotopic composition of released methane are essential for distinguishing between biogenic and thermogenic sources. Comparative analysis of carbon release rates from volcanic processes versus alternative mechanisms such as methane hydrate destabilization would inform our understanding of which processes could plausibly generate the rapid CIE onset observed in high-resolution records. The discrepancy between two-stage modeling results and known PETM hypotheses particularly for Stage 1 emissions suggests that our current framework may be missing critical processes or carbon sources. Understanding the spatial heterogeneity in responses, as evidenced by variable petrogenic carbon mobilization across latitudes, will be crucial for developing comprehensive models of this extraordinary climate event.

The persistent tension between volcanic and methane hydrate hypotheses reflects a deeper challenge in Earth system science: distinguishing between initial triggers and amplifying mechanisms. The chronological evidence linking North Atlantic Igneous Province volcanism to PETM onset provides compelling support for volcanic forcing as an initial trigger, yet carbon cycle modeling consistently demonstrates that volcanic emissions alone cannot account for the isotopic signature or carbon mass of the event. Conversely, methane hydrate dissociation can explain isotopic observations but requires an external warming trigger. This apparent contradiction dissolves when recognizing that these mechanisms likely operated sequentially and synergistically—volcanic warming crossing a threshold that destabilized marine methane reservoirs, which then amplified the initial perturbation through positive feedback.

The research gaps identified across themes converge on a single fundamental uncertainty: quantifying relative contributions and timing relationships among carbon sources. This convergence suggests that future progress depends less on improved proxy development for individual components and more on integrated frameworks that can simultaneously constrain multiple carbon fluxes. The methodological shift from descriptive stratigraphy to sophisticated numerical modeling represents movement toward such integration, yet current models remain limited by uncertain boundary conditions, particularly regarding Paleocene baseline climate states and pre-event carbon reservoir sizes.

completed_edits.merged_content:
# Discussion and Conclusions

The body of literature examining the causes of the Paleocene-Eocene Thermal Maximum reveals a fundamental transition in scientific understanding—from viewing the PETM through the lens of single trigger mechanisms to recognizing it as a cascade of interconnected Earth system processes. This systematic review of 51 papers demonstrates that the most pressing questions no longer concern *whether* volcanism or methane hydrates caused the event, but rather *how* multiple carbon sources interacted within a complex system of feedbacks and thresholds to produce the observed magnitude, timing, and recovery characteristics of this hyperthermal event approximately 56 million years ago.

## From Trigger Mechanisms to System Dynamics

The persistent tension between volcanic and methane hydrate hypotheses that permeates this literature reflects a deeper challenge in Earth system science: distinguishing between initial triggers and amplifying mechanisms. The chronological evidence linking North Atlantic Igneous Province volcanism to PETM onset provides compelling support for volcanic forcing as an initial trigger [@INWM2JEG; @JUNM4JH9; @47YBCEFM], yet carbon cycle modeling consistently demonstrates that volcanic emissions alone cannot account for the isotopic signature or carbon mass of the event [@9L889AVK]. Conversely, methane hydrate dissociation can explain isotopic observations [@DR9EQIKF; @362Y5E26] but requires an external warming trigger. This apparent contradiction dissolves when recognizing that these mechanisms likely operated sequentially and synergistically—volcanic warming crossing a threshold that destabilized marine methane reservoirs [@L358KUND; @SFHXHQXJ], which then amplified the initial perturbation through positive feedback [@9DLALEHA].

The research gaps identified across themes converge on a single fundamental uncertainty: quantifying relative contributions and timing relationships among carbon sources. Recent evidence for petrogenic organic carbon mobilization [@GTA8KIWY; @IAXX62EA] and multiple rapid carbon pulses [@WLLAETYT] adds complexity to this picture. This convergence suggests that future progress depends less on improved proxy development for individual components and more on integrated frameworks that can simultaneously constrain multiple carbon fluxes. The methodological shift from descriptive stratigraphy to sophisticated numerical modeling [@4VJB3IE6; @PZUTM9CI] represents movement toward such integration, yet current models remain limited by uncertain boundary conditions, particularly regarding Paleocene baseline climate states and pre-event carbon reservoir sizes.

## Methodological Landscape and Limitations

This review reveals significant diversity and increasing sophistication in methodological approaches. Researchers utilize geochemical proxy analyses, including stable isotope measurements [@3HVXCE9H; @JUUPBBR2; @PKCA3IP9], to reconstruct paleoenvironmental conditions and identify carbon sources. Stratigraphic and sedimentological techniques provide critical timing constraints [@PZ3PSHWV; @7B8GX4DL; @NT6V92TQ], while climate and Earth system modeling offers mechanistic insights into feedback processes [@KPX8V7AQ]. Additionally, paleontological and biostratigraphic methods [@AYMERNS9; @FB8PIYHQ] contribute to understanding biological responses that inform causation theories. This methodological pluralism strengthens the field by enabling cross-validation of findings through independent techniques.

However, the heavy reliance on carbon isotope stratigraphy as the primary constraint across nearly all research themes creates both a strength and a vulnerability. While the carbon isotope excursion provides a globally correlatable marker and direct evidence of carbon cycle perturbation, the documented spatial heterogeneity in isotopic signals—across terrestrial versus marine substrates [@9SVR963C], between surface and deep ocean environments [@PKCA3IP9], and among different carbonate preservation states—suggests that the "true" atmospheric signal remains uncertain. This uncertainty cascades through modeling efforts, where assumed CIE magnitudes directly influence calculated carbon masses and source apportionments.

More fundamentally, the predominance of proxy-based and modeling approaches, while enabling reconstruction of past events, limits mechanistic understanding of process rates and thresholds. Laboratory and experimental constraints on key processes—methane hydrate dissociation kinetics under Paleocene pressure-temperature conditions, weathering reaction rates at elevated CO₂ concentrations, or carbon isotope fractionation factors during organic matter thermogenesis—remain sparse. This gap between observational constraints and process-level understanding impedes confident application of PETM insights to modern carbon cycle perturbations.

## Key Findings and Theoretical Advances

The research reveals broad consensus on certain fundamental aspects of the PETM while highlighting persistent debates on critical questions. There is strong agreement that massive carbon release drove the event [@HGJY9YK6; @QBL568GM], yet the specific source—whether methane hydrates, organic matter combustion, volcanic activity, or some combination—remains contested. The relationship between initial triggers and subsequent feedback mechanisms continues to generate productive scientific discourse [@Y4DAR7Z9; @TBYRHSCY], with implications for understanding both past climate dynamics and potential future scenarios.

Despite these uncertainties, PETM research has advanced Earth system theory by demonstrating that negative feedbacks—particularly silicate weathering [@HP97NAV7; @3EA4CMM2; @AD2MKDMV]—can restore climate equilibrium following massive carbon release, but only over multi-millennial timescales [@US3YQCWH]. This temporal decoupling between rapid perturbation and slow recovery has profound implications for understanding both deep-time climate evolution and anthropogenic warming. The documented intensification of hydrological cycles, acceleration of weathering fluxes [@9P97Q29C], and geographic expansion of chemical weathering zones during PETM recovery provides empirical validation of theoretical feedback mechanisms central to long-term climate regulation.

Furthermore, the ocean biogeochemical responses documented during the PETM—acidification [@AG7I2UA9], deoxygenation [@C5A4BTTB; @YFSXQJH4], carbonate dissolution, and ecosystem disruptions [@NVB36TDB]—demonstrate threshold behaviors where gradual carbon addition triggers nonlinear environmental changes. These documented regime shifts challenge linear assumptions embedded in many Earth system models and highlight the importance of identifying and monitoring critical thresholds in contemporary oceans.

## Future Directions and Research Priorities

Understanding PETM causation carries profound implications for anticipating Earth system responses to rapid carbon perturbations [@G5QN8JFL; @RV2M2PC2]. As contemporary carbon emissions proceed at rates potentially exceeding PETM conditions, resolving these debates becomes increasingly urgent for informing climate projections and policy decisions.

Advancing beyond current understanding requires three parallel developments. First, expanded high-resolution terrestrial archives [@9SVR963C] with robust chronological control [@PZ3PSHWV] would reduce dependence on marine carbonate records affected by dissolution and diagenesis. Second, integrated modeling frameworks that couple carbon cycle dynamics with climate physics, ocean biogeochemistry, and sediment diagenesis could simultaneously constrain multiple processes rather than treating them independently. Third, sustained efforts to quantify Paleocene boundary conditions—particularly methane hydrate reservoir distributions [@TBYRHSCY] and baseline ocean chemistry—would reduce the parameter space in which models currently operate.

The debates identified in this review highlight where future research should concentrate efforts, particularly in obtaining higher-resolution records that can resolve the onset duration [@NT6V92TQ; @7B8GX4DL], characterizing volcanic versus biogenic carbon contributions [@JUNM4JH9; @ASJD2TES], and integrating evidence across multiple proxy systems [@7U76VLJY; @LVFPSGAY].

Most critically, future research must grapple with equifinality: the likelihood that multiple combinations of processes could produce observationally similar outcomes. Progress may ultimately depend less on achieving perfect proxy resolution and more on developing frameworks that can quantify irreducible uncertainties and identify which aspects of PETM causation can be confidently constrained versus those that remain fundamentally ambiguous given available evidence. This mature understanding—recognizing both what we know and the limits of what we can know—represents the most valuable contribution PETM research can make to understanding Earth's climate system and its response to rapid carbon cycle perturbations.
